verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: INTRODUCTION TO THE TREATISE THAT NO ONE CAN HARM THE MAN WHO DOES NOT INJURE HIMSELF.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: This very beautiful treatise was composed when St. Chrysostom was in exile, probably not long before his death, and was sent with a letter to his great friend the deaconess Olympias in Constantinople.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Plato in the 10th book of his Dialogue called the “Republic” employs an argument to prove the immortality of the soul, so nearly resembling a portion of this treatise that I can scarcely doubt St. Chrysostom had it in his mind.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: The following is the passage in the Platonic dialogue as rendered in the excellent translation of Messrs.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Davies & Vaughan.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: I omit a few sentences here and there.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: “Have you not learned, I asked, that our soul is immortal and never dies?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: He looked at me and said in amazement.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: No really I have not: but can you maintain this doctrine?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Yes as I am an honest man, I replied, and I think you could also.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: It is quite easy to do it.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Proceed by all means.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: So you call one thing good and another evil?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: And do we hold the same opinion as to the meaning of two terms?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: What opinion do you hold?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: I hold that the term evil comprises everything that destroys and corrupts, and the term good everything that preserves and benefits.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Again; do you maintain that everything has its evil and its good?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Do you say for example that the eyes are liable to the evil of ophthalmia, the entire body to disease, corn to mildew, timber to rot, copper and iron to rust or in other words that almost everything is liable to some connatural evil and malady?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: And is it not the case that, whenever an object is attacked by one of these maladies it is impaired; and in the end completely broken up and destroyed by it?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Hence everything is destroyed by its own connatural evil and vice: otherwise if it be not destroyed by this, there is nothing else that can corrupt it.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: For that which is good will never destroy anything, nor yet that which is neither good nor evil.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: If then we can find among existing things one which is liable to a particular evil which can indeed mar it, but cannot break it up or destroy it, shall we not be at once certain that a thing so constituted can never perish?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: That would be a reasonable conclusion.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Well then is not the soul liable to a malady which renders it evil?

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Certainly it is: all those things which we were lately discussing—injustice, intemperance, cowardice, and ignorance—produce that result.”

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Then having proved that although these things injure the soul they do not actually destroy it he proceeds.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: “Well, it is irrational to suppose that a thing can be destroyed by the depravity of another thing, though it cannot be destroyed by its own.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: True it is irrational.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Yes it is: for you must remember that we do not imagine that a body is to be destroyed by the proper depravity of its food whatever that may be, whether mouldiness or rottenness or anything else.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: But if the depravity of the food itself produces in the body a disorder proper to the body, we shall assert that the body has been destroyed by its food remotely, but by its own proper vice or disease, immediately: and we shall always disclaim the notion that the body can be corrupted by the depravity of its food which is a different thing from the body—that is to say, the notion that the body can be corrupted by an alien evil without the introduction of its own native evil.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: You are perfectly correct.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Then according to the same reasoning I continued, unless depravity of body introduces into the soul depravity of soul let us never suppose that the soul can be destroyed by an alien evil without the presence of its own peculiar disease: for that would be to suppose that one thing can be destroyed by the evil of another thing.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: That is a reasonable statement.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Well then let us either refute this doctrine and point out our mistake or else, so long as it remains unrefuted, let us never assert that a fever or any other disease, or fatal violence, or even the act of cutting up the entire body into the smallest possible pieces can have any tendency to destroy the soul, until it has been demonstrated that in consequence of this treatment of the body the soul itself becomes more unjust and more unholy.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: For so long as a thing is exempt from its own proper evil, while an evil foreign to it appears in another subject, let us not allow it to be said that this thing whether it be a soul or anything else is in danger of being destroyed.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Well, certainly no one will prove that the souls of the dying become more unjust in consequence of death.”

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Here follows a passage to prove that even injustice does not destroy the soul, after which he proceeds,

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: “Surely then when the soul cannot be killed and destroyed by its own depravity and its own evil, hardly will the evil which is charged with the destruction of another thing destroy a soul or anything else beyond its own appropriate object.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Hardly: at least that is the natural inference.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: Hence, as it is destroyed by no evil at all, whether foreign to it or its own, it is clear that the soul must be always existing, and therefore immortal.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: If any one will compare this extract with chapters 2 to 6 in the following treatise he cannot fail to be struck by the similarity of thought and language, although in the latter case it is more apparent in the original than it can be in translation.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: The aim of the two writers is not indeed identical: Chrysostom’s object is to prove that nothing can really injure a man except sin—depravity of soul—Plato begins by proving this, and proceeds to maintain that if even that which corrupts the soul cannot actually destroy it the soul must be imperishable.

verses.book: A Treatise to Prove that No One Can Harm the Man Who Does Not Injure Himself.
verses.text: They employ the same argument, only Plato carries it a step further than Chrysostom.
